animation.graph_init: Preparing to start BFS, initializing all distances to ∞
animation.graph_start: Add the start node {{start}} to the queue (distance: 0)
animation.dequeue: While the queue is not empty, dequeue {{curr}} (distance: {{dist}})
animation.explore_some: Exploring neighbors of {{curr}}, found {{count}} unvisited node(s)
animation.explore_none: Exploring neighbors of {{curr}}, all neighbors have already been visited
animation.visit_neighbor: Found unvisited neighbor {{neighbor}}, adding it to the queue
animation.not_found: The queue is empty, destination cannot be reached
animation.grid_blocked: The start or destination is blocked by a wall, cannot begin

animation.grid_start: Initialize the start cell {{start}} and add it to the queue
animation.grid_visit_neighbor: Found neighbor {{neighbor}}, adding it to the queue

bfs-flood-fill-paint.title: Paint Bucket Tool: The BFS Flood Fill Behind Photoshop

sections.heading: Every Time You Click the Paint Bucket, BFS Runs Once
sections.content: In image editing software, the paint bucket (Fill) is one of the most intuitive tools: click on a pixel, and the connected region of the same color gets replaced by a new color. Behind this, it's typically not a recursive stack overflow, but a queue-implemented Flood Fill — essentially BFS: starting from the clicked pixel, sequentially adding four-connected neighbors with matching color to the queue, expanding outward layer by layer, until no more cells can be filled.

sections.heading: Why BFS Instead of Just Paint Randomly
sections.content: Depth-First Search (DFS) could also fill the area, but BFS expands in "near-to-far" concentric wave fronts — visually like ripples in water, and easier to parallelize and debug. With multiple starting points filling simultaneously, it becomes multi-source BFS: each color maintains its own wave front, advancing synchronously on the global timeline — consistent with the "multiple colors expanding simultaneously" model in this interactive canvas.

sections.heading: From Tool to Exam Problem
sections.content: Flood Fill is the prototype of classic interview and competitive programming problems (such as "Number of Islands" and "Color Fill"). Understanding "connectivity + queue" also connects to practical topics like scan lines and GPU filling. The mini-program below uses Python to output pattern and size metadata; the browser rasterizes the edges into an 80×80 pixel grid using Canvas 2D. You select a color and click on the webpage, the actual fill order is calculated by TypeScript-side BFS, and you can use playback controls to play, pause, and scrub through steps to observe how the algorithm overwrites the canvas cell by cell.

bfs-flood-fill-paint.pythonDemoTitle: BFS Flood Fill — Pixel Art Coloring Game

relatedProblems.994: Multi-source BFS: all rotten oranges spread simultaneously, each round represents one minute
relatedProblems.127: Unweighted shortest path: treat each word as a node and one-letter-apart as an edge; BFS finds the shortest transformation sequence
relatedProblems.542: Multi-source BFS: expand from all 0-cells simultaneously, recording each 1-cell's distance to the nearest 0
relatedProblems.286: Multi-source BFS: start from all gates (0) simultaneously, fill each empty room with its distance to the nearest gate
relatedProblems.1091: BFS shortest path: move in 8 directions in a binary matrix; BFS layer expansion guarantees the shortest path
